title: Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in ArcGIS Online | Stats NZ
content:
## Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in ArcGIS Online
16 October 2025, 11:00am
This step-by-step guide shows you how to retrieve data in ArcGIS Online using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API.
The instructions below are adapted from Geoenable SDMX with Data Pipelines .
You can use the Data Pipelines interface to request data from the Aotearoa Data Explorer API, perform data preparation operations, and write the data to a feature layer that can be used anywhere in the ArcGIS system.
Use of Data Pipelines r equires an ArcGIS Online account and will consume credits . To publish a feature service, you will need at least a Creator account.
If you don’t want to use Data Pipelines, you can download CSV files from Aotearoa Data Explorer and import them into ArcGIS rather than using the API. That method is not covered here.

## Step 1: Get an API key
Sign up to Stats NZ’s API Portal if you haven’t already. In the portal, subscribe to the Aotearoa Data Explorer API and generate a primary key. You’ll need this key in step 5.
API Portal explains how to sign up and create and manage subscription keys.

## Step 2: Determine the URL for the data you want
The data you want needs to be defined in a URL.
An easy way to determine a URL is to use the Aotearoa Data Explorer browser to find and customise a table. Then select the Developer API option above the table (2a in the screenshot below) and copy the data query URL (2b).
Selecting data for just one geographic level is recommended so it can be easily joined with boundaries. Quick start guide to Aotearoa Data Explorer includes a section called Filter what data you see in a table if you need help selecting one geographic level in the Aotearoa Data Explorer browser.
Aotearoa Data Explorer API – user guide has more detail about URLs.

### Step 3: Add the format type to your URL
Append ‘&format=csvfilewithlabels’ to the end of your URL.
This specifies that the data should be returned in CSV format, which is required by Data Pipelines, rather than the default of XML.
Continuing the example from step 2, your URL should now look like this:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STATSNZ,CEN23_TBT_005,1.0/aa1+dd02+do1+ft011 +he1+nbmea.12+13+14+15+16+17+18+99+01+02+03+04+05+06+07+08+09.2023? dimensionAtObservation=AllDimensions&format=csvfilewithlabels
You’ll need this in step 5.

## Step 4: Create a data pipeline
In ArcGIS Online, launch Data Pipelines from the app launcher in the upper-right corner and click Create data pipeline .

## Step 5: Bring in geographic boundaries
To bring in geographic boundaries, click Inputs from the menu on the left, scroll down to the ArcGIS section, and click Feature layer .
Click the My content drop-down menu, then click Living Atlas .
From Living Atlas, search for the New Zealand boundaries matching those from your API query. For this example, use the current (2025) regional council boundaries, which match the 2023 boundaries used for 2023 Census data.

## Step 6: Bring in the API as an input
Click Inputs from the menu on the left again, click Public URL , and paste in the URL from step 3. From the Response format drop-down menu, select CSV or delimited .
Under Custom headers , enter ‘Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key’ in the Name field and your primary key from step 1 in Value .

## Step 7: Manage fields
Scroll down in the Public URL configuration menu and click Configure schema . Here you can change field types, remove, and rename fields. You may need to change the field type of any ID codes to String to retain leading zeroes for merging with boundaries in step 10.

## Step 8: Prepare the API data for joining
To join data with the boundaries you need a common field. This will usually be the area codes.
If the codes don’t match perfectly – for example, the codes from the API include a prefix – you may be able to use the Calculate field tool to modify the codes for a match. Click Tools on the left then Calculate field to use this functionality.
Merging on area labels may be possible if you cannot use the codes.

## Step 9: Pivot the API data for joining
This step changes the data from a long dataset to a wide dataset. Doing so creates one geographic feature with the variables as separate fields, instead of a separate geographic feature for each variable.
You may need to create a new concatenated variable to define the columns if your dataset contains more than one non-geographic variable. Use the Calculate new field tool to concatenate multiple variables into a single new variable.
In this example, we created a new variable called Variable_year by combining Variable_codes with Year.
Use the Pivot tool and specify the following to create the wide dataset:
- Input dataset is the name of the dataset you want to transform.
- Input fields are the fields that will continue to define the rows. Include the geographic field as an input field.
- Pivot field is the variable that will be pivoted to define the columns. We use Variable_year here from the concatenation step above.
- Value field is the field with the numbers, which in Aotearoa Data Explorer is OBS_VALUE.
Pivot – ArcGIS Data Pipelines has more about the pivot function.

## Step 10: Join the data and boundaries
Under Tools , click Join .
The Target dataset is the data file from step 9, and the Join dataset is the intermediate boundary file from step 5.
Leave the defaults of Join one to many and Inner join . Under Attribute relationship , click Use matching fields and choose the fields you are using to join (for example, the area code fields).

## Step 11: Preview the data
Preview the output by clicking the map button on the left to confirm that the data now has a spatial component.

## Step 12: Select output options
Click Outputs then select Feature layer to create an output.
For Output method , click Create because this is the first time you are generating this feature layer. If you were setting this up to run on a recurring schedule when the source data is updated, you could select Replace .
Type a name in the Output name text box.
Save the project from the menu on the left, and click Run at the top right.
Now you have an ArcGIS Online feature service, which you can bring into Map Viewer or open in ArcGIS Pro for visualisation, analysis, and sharing.
Once you build a data pipeline, you can schedule it to run automatically at a predetermined interval.
